back: A data structure composed of a set of vertices (nodes) and edges (links) that connect pairs of these vertices. It is used to model relationships in complex systems.
Also known as a node, it represents an entity within a graph and serves as the fundamental unit of the structure.
A connection between two vertices. In a directed graph, edges indicate a specific direction of relationship.
A numerical value assigned to an edge representing cost, distance, or another measure impacting traversal.
A sequence of vertices connected by edges, defined by starting at one vertex and traversing through edges to reach another.
A path that begins and ends at the same vertex, containing at least one edge and no repeated edges or internal vertices.
A graph with no cycles, meaning it is impossible to return to a starting vertex by following edges.
A graph where edges have a specific direction, indicating a one-way relationship from one vertex to another.
A directed graph containing no cycles, often used for task scheduling and sequences where tasks do not repeat.
A type of acyclic graph with a designated root vertex where all other vertices are connected by exactly one path to that root.
A square matrix used to represent a graph where elements indicate whether pairs of vertices are adjacent.
A collection of lists used to represent a graph, where each list describes the neighbors of a specific vertex.
A search algorithm that explores all neighbor nodes at the current depth prior to moving on to nodes at the next depth level.
A sequence of moves on a chessboard where a knight visits every square exactly once.
A traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
A tree data structure used for efficiently locating specific keys within a set through insertion, deletion, and search operations.
A technique designed to solve problems quickly or find approximate solutions when classic methods are too slow or infeasible.
A heuristic for the knight's tour where the knight always moves to the square with the fewest onward moves.
A collection of trees formed during a depth-first search, where each tree corresponds to a set of vertices explored contiguously.
A DFS property stating that discovery and finish time intervals for any two vertices are either entirely disjoint or nested.
A linear ordering of vertices in a directed graph where for every edge uv, vertex u comes before v.
Maximal subgraphs where every pair of vertices has a directed path to and from each other.
The challenge of finding a path between two vertices such that the sum of the weights of the edges is minimized.
An algorithm for finding the shortest paths between nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights.
A data structure where each element has a priority, and elements are served based on that priority rather than just arrival order.
A networking technique where a packet is sent to every outgoing link except the one it arrived on.
A subset of edges connecting all vertices without cycles and with the minimum total edge weight.
An algorithm that finds a minimum spanning tree by growing a tree one edge at a time from a starting vertex.
A class of algorithms that build a solution by always choosing the next piece that offers the most immediate benefit.
